questions.stem:
A medical student is preparing to perform a lumbar puncture on a 45-year-old man with suspected meningitis. She identifies the correct interspace by palpating a surface landmark at the level of the iliac crests.

Which vertebral level does this surface landmark correspond to?
options: L2
L3
L4
L5
S1
questions.explanation: The supracristal plane (Tuffier's line), connecting the highest points of the iliac crests, corresponds to the L4 vertebral level. Lumbar puncture is performed at L3/L4 or L4/L5, safely below the conus medullaris which ends at L1-L2 in adults.

questions.stem:
A surgeon is planning to insert a chest drain for a large right pleural effusion. She identifies the safe triangle for drain insertion.

Which of the following best describes the boundaries of the safe triangle?
options: Medial border of pectoralis major, medial border of latissimus dorsi, above the 3rd rib
Lateral border of pectoralis major, lateral border of latissimus dorsi, above the nipple line
Midclavicular line, midaxillary line, below the 2nd intercostal space
Midaxillary line, posterior axillary line, below the 5th intercostal space
Medial border of trapezius, lateral border of SCM, above the clavicle
questions.explanation: The safe triangle is bounded anteriorly by the lateral border of pectoralis major, posteriorly by the lateral border of latissimus dorsi, and inferiorly by the 5th intercostal space. The drain is inserted above the upper border of the lower rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle (vein, artery, nerve) in the costal groove.

questions.stem:
On a standard PA chest X-ray, a medical student is asked to identify the borders of the cardiac silhouette.

Which structure forms the right lower border of the cardiac silhouette on a PA chest X-ray?
options: Right ventricle
Superior vena cava
Right atrium
Left atrium
Inferior vena cava
questions.explanation: On a PA chest X-ray: the right border is formed superiorly by the SVC and inferiorly by the right atrium. The left border is formed superiorly by the aortic knuckle, then pulmonary trunk, left atrial appendage, and inferiorly by the left ventricle.

questions.stem:
A radiologist is reviewing an MRI of the knee following a sports injury. She reports that the anterior cruciate ligament appears as a structure of uniformly low signal intensity on T2-weighted sequences.

What does low signal intensity (dark appearance) on T2-weighted MRI indicate in ligamentous tissue?
options: High free water content indicating acute inflammation
Dense fibrous tissue with low mobile proton content — normal appearance of intact ligament
Calcification of the ligament
Haemorrhage within the ligament
Lipomatous infiltration of the ligament
questions.explanation: Ligaments and tendons are composed of densely packed collagen fibres with very little free water. This gives uniformly low signal (dark) on both T1 and T2-weighted MRI. A torn or acutely injured ligament shows increased T2 signal (bright) due to oedema and haemorrhage within the disrupted fibres.

questions.stem:
A CT scan of the abdomen at the level of L2 shows a large vessel anterior to the vertebral body and slightly to the left of the midline. A second vessel lies immediately to its right.

What are these two vessels?
options: Portal vein and superior mesenteric vein
Inferior vena cava (right) and abdominal aorta (left)
Left and right common iliac arteries
Superior mesenteric artery (left) and inferior vena cava (right)
Left renal artery and right renal vein
questions.explanation: On cross-sectional CT at L2, the abdominal aorta lies slightly to the left of midline anterior to the vertebral body, and the IVC lies immediately to its right. The aorta bifurcates at L4 into the common iliac arteries. The IVC is formed at L5 by the union of the common iliac veins.

questions.stem:
A deep dissection of the anterior neck shows a large purple-coloured vessel running lateral to the common carotid artery within the carotid sheath.

Which structure is indicated?
options: External jugular vein
Anterior jugular vein
Internal jugular vein
Subclavian vein
Brachiocephalic vein
questions.explanation: The internal jugular vein runs within the carotid sheath lateral to the common carotid artery, with the vagus nerve lying posteriorly between the two. It joins the subclavian vein to form the brachiocephalic vein posterior to the sternoclavicular joint. It is the preferred site for central venous cannulation.

questions.stem:
A lateral neck dissection shows a purple-coloured vessel in the superficial neck, lateral and superficial to the SCM muscle. This vessel is used as a clinical indicator of central venous pressure.

What is it?
options: Internal jugular vein
Common carotid artery
External jugular vein
Anterior jugular vein
Facial vein
questions.explanation: The external jugular vein crosses the sternocleidomastoid muscle superficially and is visible through the skin — it is the vessel assessed clinically for jugular venous pressure (JVP). It runs from the angle of the mandible to the midpoint of the clavicle where it drains into the subclavian vein.

questions.stem:
A dissection of the anterior neck shows a nerve running alongside the common carotid artery, medial to the internal jugular vein. This nerve is the principal parasympathetic supply to the thoracic and abdominal viscera.

What is it?
options: Phrenic nerve
Hypoglossal nerve
Sympathetic trunk
Vagus nerve
Glossopharyngeal nerve
questions.explanation: The vagus nerve (CN X) runs within the carotid sheath, posterior to and between the common carotid artery and internal jugular vein. It provides parasympathetic supply to the heart, lungs, oesophagus, and abdominal viscera to the splenic flexure. The phrenic nerve runs on the anterior surface of scalenus anterior, lateral to the carotid sheath.

questions.stem:
A lumbar vertebra is viewed from a superior-oblique angle. Structure A is the large cylindrical anteriorly-placed mass of bone.

What is structure A, and what does it primarily contribute to in the spine?
options: Spinous process — attachment of spinal ligaments
Transverse process — attachment of erector spinae
Pedicle — forming the lateral wall of the vertebral foramen
Vertebral body — weight-bearing and shock absorption
Lamina — forming the posterior wall of the vertebral canal
questions.explanation: The vertebral body is the large anterior cylindrical mass of cancellous bone. It is the primary weight-bearing component of the spine. The intervertebral disc sits between adjacent bodies, distributing load and allowing movement. Crush fractures of the vertebral body are a major complication of osteoporosis.

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents with severe right leg pain radiating from the buttock to the lateral calf and dorsum of the foot following heavy lifting. He has weakness of right big toe extension and numbness over the dorsum of the right foot.

Which nerve root is most likely compressed, and at which disc level?
options: L4 root — L3/L4 disc
L5 root — L4/L5 disc
S1 root — L5/S1 disc
L3 root — L2/L3 disc
S2 root — L5/S1 disc
questions.explanation: L5 root compression (from L4/L5 disc herniation) causes: pain along the lateral leg to the dorsum of the foot; weakness of extensor hallucis longus (big toe extension) and tibialis anterior; sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and first web space; ankle reflex is preserved (S1). S1 root (L5/S1 disc) causes plantar flexion weakness, lateral sole sensory loss, and a lost ankle jerk.

questions.stem:
A posterior dissection of the hip and gluteal region shows a large muscle forming the bulk of the buttock, with the sciatic nerve visible deep to it.

What is this muscle and what is its nerve supply?
options: Gluteus medius — superior gluteal nerve
Piriformis — direct branches from sacral plexus S1-S2
Gluteus maximus — inferior gluteal nerve
Biceps femoris — tibial division of sciatic nerve
Gluteus minimus — superior gluteal nerve
questions.explanation: Gluteus maximus is the largest and most superficial gluteal muscle, supplied by the inferior gluteal nerve (L5, S1, S2). It is the primary hip extensor and also contributes to lateral rotation. Intramuscular injections into the buttock must be given into the upper outer quadrant to avoid the sciatic nerve and inferior gluteal nerve.

questions.stem:
A 28-year-old female gymnast presents with anterior knee pain. MRI shows disruption of the ligament preventing anterior translation of the tibia on the femur.

Which ligament is damaged, and which clinical test is most sensitive for its assessment?
options: Posterior cruciate ligament — posterior draw test
Medial collateral ligament — valgus stress test at 30°
Anterior cruciate ligament — Lachman's test
Lateral collateral ligament — varus stress test
Patellar tendon — Clarke's test
questions.explanation: The ACL prevents anterior translation of the tibia on the femur. Lachman's test (anterior draw with knee at 20-30° of flexion) is the most sensitive clinical test (sensitivity >80%). Haemarthrosis develops within 2 hours of ACL injury, earlier than other knee ligament injuries.

questions.stem:
A 55-year-old man develops winging of his right scapula following a right axillary lymph node dissection. The medial border of his right scapula protrudes posteriorly when he pushes against a wall.

Which nerve has been damaged and what is its root value?
options: Axillary nerve — C5, C6
Suprascapular nerve — C5, C6
Long thoracic nerve — C5, C6, C7
Thoracodorsal nerve — C6, C7, C8
Medial pectoral nerve — C8, T1
questions.explanation: The long thoracic nerve (C5, C6, C7) supplies serratus anterior, which holds the medial border of the scapula against the thoracic wall and rotates the glenoid upward for full arm elevation. Damage during axillary dissection causes winging of the scapula. Without serratus anterior, full arm elevation above 90° is also impaired.

questions.stem: In intercostal procedures, which relationship of the neurovascular bundle to the rib must be remembered to avoid injury?
options: The bundle runs on the superior border of the rib — insert above the lower rib
The bundle runs in the costal groove on the inferior border of the rib — insert above the upper border of the lower rib
The bundle runs in the midpoint of the intercostal space
The bundle runs on both borders of each rib equally
The bundle runs only on the posterior surface of the rib
questions.explanation: The intercostal neurovascular bundle (VAN — Vein, Artery, Nerve from superior to inferior) runs in the costal groove along the inferior border of each rib. To avoid the bundle during thoracocentesis or chest drain insertion, the needle should be inserted just above the upper border of the lower rib.

questions.stem:
A blue-coloured vessel runs superficially along the medial aspect of the leg from the foot to the thigh.

What is this vessel and where does it drain?
options: Small saphenous vein — drains into popliteal vein
Femoral vein — drains into external iliac vein
Great saphenous vein — drains into femoral vein at the saphenofemoral junction
Perforating vein — drains into deep femoral vein
Posterior tibial vein — drains into popliteal vein
questions.explanation: The great (long) saphenous vein is the longest vein in the body, running from the medial side of the dorsal venous arch of the foot along the medial aspect of the leg and thigh, draining into the femoral vein at the saphenofemoral junction (4 cm below and lateral to the pubic tubercle). It is used for venepuncture, CABG grafting, and varicose vein surgery.

questions.stem: A large nerve emerges from below piriformis and descends in the posterior compartment of the thigh. A patient sustains a posterior hip dislocation. Which component of this nerve is most commonly affected, and what would be the resulting deficit?
options: Tibial division — loss of plantarflexion and toe flexion
Common fibular division — foot drop and loss of eversion with sensory loss over dorsum of foot
Both divisions equally — complete loss of all foot and ankle movement
Femoral nerve — loss of knee extension
Obturator nerve — loss of hip adduction
questions.explanation: The sciatic nerve is at risk in posterior hip dislocation. The common fibular division is more vulnerable due to its more lateral position. Injury causes foot drop (loss of dorsiflexion and eversion), high-stepping gait, and sensory loss over the anterolateral leg and dorsum of the foot.

questions.stem: A red artery and purple vein pass through the adductor (Hunter's) canal in the medial thigh. Through which structure does the femoral artery exit this canal to become the popliteal artery?
options: Obturator foramen
Greater sciatic foramen
Adductor hiatus in adductor magnus
Femoral ring
Saphenous opening (fossa ovalis)
questions.explanation: The femoral artery travels through the adductor canal, then passes through the adductor hiatus — an opening in the tendinous insertion of adductor magnus — to enter the popliteal fossa as the popliteal artery. The saphenous nerve (sensory branch of femoral nerve) travels with the artery through the canal.

questions.stem:
A 30-year-old cyclist sustains a fracture of the neck of the fibula. He has foot drop and cannot evert the foot.

Why is this nerve particularly vulnerable at this location?
options: It passes through the posterior compartment with no soft tissue protection
It winds directly around the neck of the fibula superficially, with minimal soft tissue coverage, closely applied to bone
It is tethered to the fibula by a strong fascial band that tears with fracture
It enters the anterior compartment through a fibrous ring that constricts with fracture
It runs in the peroneal groove posterior to the lateral malleolus
questions.explanation: The common fibular nerve winds around the neck of the fibula in a superficial position, closely applied to the periosteum with minimal soft tissue protection. It is vulnerable to fracture, direct trauma, prolonged pressure, or compression during surgery. It divides into deep fibular nerve (anterior compartment — dorsiflexion) and superficial fibular nerve (lateral compartment — eversion).

questions.stem: A patient has the median nerve compressed at the wrist. Which of the following deficits would they NOT have?
options: Wasting of abductor pollicis brevis
Sensory loss over the palmar surface of the thumb, index and middle fingers
Weakness of opponens pollicis
Wasting of the hypothenar eminence
Positive Tinel's sign at the wrist
questions.explanation: The median nerve passes through the carpal tunnel. Compression causes thenar eminence wasting (APB, opponens pollicis, superficial FPB — LOAF muscles), sensory loss over the radial 3.5 digits on the palmar surface, and positive Tinel's/Phalen's tests. The hypothenar eminence is supplied by the ulnar nerve — NOT affected in carpal tunnel syndrome.

questions.stem: A patient sustains a deep laceration at the wrist severing the median nerve. Which specific muscle would test most sensitively for this injury?
options: Flexor digitorum profundus to the index finger
Flexor carpi ulnaris
Abductor pollicis brevis
Flexor carpi radialis
Palmaris longus
questions.explanation: Abductor pollicis brevis (APB) is the most reliable muscle to test for median nerve injury at the wrist. It is supplied exclusively by the recurrent (thenar) branch of the median nerve, which arises just distal to the flexor retinaculum. APB abducts the thumb perpendicular to the palm — tested against resistance.

questions.stem: A nerve enters the hand on the medial side of the wrist, passing through Guyon's canal. Injury to this nerve at the wrist causes clawing of which fingers, and why are those specific fingers affected?
options: All four fingers — all lumbricals are paralysed
Index and middle fingers — lumbricals 1 and 2 are paralysed
Ring and little fingers — lumbricals 3 and 4 and all interossei are paralysed, allowing FDP and EDC to dominate
Little finger only — hypothenar muscles are the only muscles affected
Thumb and index finger — adductor pollicis and first dorsal interosseous are paralysed
questions.explanation: Ulnar nerve injury at the wrist causes clawing of the ring and little fingers. The interossei and lumbricals 3 and 4 (ulnar nerve) normally flex the MCP joints and extend the IP joints. Index and middle fingers are less affected because their lumbricals (1 and 2) are supplied by the median nerve. This is the ulnar paradox: wrist injury causes more clawing than elbow injury because FDP is also affected at the elbow.

questions.stem: A patient has a fracture at the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Which nerve is most at risk, and what is its anatomical relationship to this bone at this point?
options: Radial nerve — lies in the spiral groove on the posterior surface
Median nerve — passes anterior to the medial epicondyle in the cubital fossa
Ulnar nerve — passes posterior to the medial epicondyle in the cubital tunnel
Anterior interosseous nerve — passes between the heads of pronator teres
Musculocutaneous nerve — passes lateral to the biceps tendon
questions.explanation: The ulnar nerve passes posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus in the cubital tunnel. It is palpable here and is the most commonly injured nerve at the elbow. Injury causes clawing of ring and little fingers, hypothenar wasting, interossei wasting, and sensory loss over the ulnar 1.5 digits and medial palm.

questions.stem:
A large red vessel runs vertically on the left of midline anterior to the vertebral column, and a blue vessel lies immediately to its right.

At which vertebral level does the aorta bifurcate, and what does it divide into?
options: L2 — left and right renal arteries
L3 — superior and inferior mesenteric arteries
L4 — left and right common iliac arteries
L5 — left and right external iliac arteries
T12 — coeliac trunk and superior mesenteric artery
questions.explanation: The abdominal aorta bifurcates at L4 into the left and right common iliac arteries. Each then divides at the sacroiliac joint into the internal iliac artery (pelvic viscera and perineum) and external iliac artery (passes under the inguinal ligament to become the femoral artery). The IVC is formed at L5.

questions.stem:
During a hysterectomy, the surgeon ligates the uterine arteries bilaterally. A post-operative complication reveals bilateral ureteric injury.

Why is the ureter particularly at risk during ligation of the uterine artery?
options: The ureter runs superior to the uterine artery at the pelvic brim
The ureter crosses the uterine artery laterally at the level of the broad ligament
The ureter passes immediately inferior to the uterine artery at the lateral fornix of the cervix — water under the bridge
The ureter runs within the broad ligament alongside the uterine artery
The ureter is tethered to the uterine artery by peritoneal folds
questions.explanation: The ureter passes beneath the uterine artery at the lateral fornix of the cervix — water (ureter) under the bridge (uterine artery). The uterine artery crosses superior to the ureter approximately 1-2 cm lateral to the cervix. This is the most common site of iatrogenic ureteric injury.

questions.stem:
A 68-year-old man with benign prostatic hyperplasia has obstruction in the transitional zone of the prostate.

Which zone of the prostate is most commonly the site of BPH, and which zone is most commonly the site of prostate cancer?
options: Peripheral zone — BPH; central zone — prostate cancer
Transitional zone — BPH; peripheral zone — prostate cancer
Central zone — BPH; transitional zone — prostate cancer
Peripheral zone — BPH; transitional zone — prostate cancer
Transitional zone — BPH; central zone — prostate cancer
questions.explanation: The transitional zone (periurethral glands) is the primary site of BPH — expansion compresses the urethra causing LUTS. The peripheral zone (comprising ~70% of glandular tissue) is the primary site of prostate cancer (~75% of cancers), which is why DRE can palpate posterior prostate cancers.

questions.stem: During insertion of a pleural drain in the 5th intercostal space, at which position in the interspace should the needle pass to avoid the neurovascular bundle?
options: Through the middle of the intercostal space to avoid both ribs
Immediately below the upper rib, in the superior part of the space
Just above the upper border of the lower rib
Posterior to the midaxillary line only
Anywhere in the space as the neurovascular bundle is only in the posterior intercostal space
questions.explanation: The intercostal neurovascular bundle (VAN — Vein, Artery, Nerve from superior to inferior) runs in the costal groove along the inferior border of each rib. The needle must be inserted just above the upper border of the lower rib. The nerve is the most inferior of the three structures and therefore most vulnerable if the needle tracks superiorly.

questions.stem:
The oesophagus passes through the diaphragm accompanied by the vagal trunks.

At which vertebral level is the oesophageal hiatus, and what additional structures pass through it?
options: T8 — inferior vena cava and right phrenic nerve
T10 — oesophagus, anterior and posterior vagal trunks, and oesophageal branches of the left gastric vessels
T12 — aorta, thoracic duct, and azygos vein
T10 — oesophagus and both phrenic nerves
T8 — oesophagus and thoracic duct
questions.explanation: Diaphragmatic openings: T8 = IVC (and right phrenic nerve); T10 = oesophageal hiatus (oesophagus + anterior and posterior vagal trunks + oesophageal branches of left gastric vessels); T12 = aortic hiatus (aorta + thoracic duct + azygos vein). Mnemonic: I ate (8) ten (10) Eggs At twelve (12).

questions.stem:
A surgeon harvests the left internal thoracic (mammary) artery for CABG anastomosis to the left anterior descending artery.

From which vessel does the internal thoracic artery originate?
options: Axillary artery
Common carotid artery
Thyrocervical trunk
Subclavian artery
Costocervical trunk
questions.explanation: The internal thoracic (mammary) artery originates from the inferior surface of the subclavian artery, medial to the thyrocervical trunk. It descends parasternally giving intercostal branches and terminates as the superior epigastric and musculophrenic arteries. The left ITA is the preferred conduit for CABG to the LAD with excellent long-term patency.

questions.stem:
Both lungs are viewed from the front. The right lung has an additional fissure not present in the left lung.

What is this fissure called, and what lobe does it delineate?
options: Oblique fissure — separating the upper and lower lobes
Horizontal fissure — separating the upper and middle lobes of the right lung
Cardiac fissure — accommodating the cardiac notch
Mediastinal fissure — separating the medial and lateral segments
Accessory fissure — separating the superior segment of the lower lobe
questions.explanation: The right lung has two fissures — the oblique fissure (dividing upper+middle from lower lobe) and the horizontal (transverse) fissure (dividing upper from middle lobe). The left lung has only the oblique fissure plus the lingula. On CXR, the horizontal fissure is visible on the right as a horizontal line at the level of the anterior end of the 4th costal cartilage.

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old tall slim man presents with sudden onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness. On examination, the right side is hyper-resonant with absent breath sounds.

Where would you perform thoracocentesis to aspirate a pleural effusion, and why is this site chosen?
options: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line — avoids lung and major vessels
9th intercostal space, posterior to midaxillary line, above the upper border of the 10th rib — within the costodiaphragmatic recess
5th intercostal space, midclavicular line — within the safe triangle
1st intercostal space, midaxillary line — above the lung apex
7th intercostal space, midsternal line — avoids the heart
questions.explanation: Pleural fluid accumulates first in the costodiaphragmatic recess — the lowest point of the pleural cavity. Thoracocentesis is performed in the 9th intercostal space in the posterior midaxillary line, above the upper border of the 10th rib. Ultrasound guidance is now standard. The 2nd ICS midclavicular line is used for emergency needle decompression of tension pneumothorax.

questions.stem: Which cartilage of the larynx is the only complete cartilaginous ring in the entire airway, and what is its clinical significance?
options: Thyroid cartilage — forms the laryngeal prominence (Adam's apple)
Epiglottis — protects the airway during swallowing
Arytenoid cartilages — anchor the posterior vocal folds
Cricoid cartilage — forms the subglottic cricothyroid space; used for emergency airway access
First tracheal ring — can be used as landmark for tracheostomy
questions.explanation: The cricoid cartilage is the only complete ring in the airway (tracheal rings are C-shaped). In children it is the narrowest part of the airway — foreign bodies lodge here. The cricothyroid membrane (between thyroid and cricoid cartilages) is the site of emergency cricothyroidotomy in cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate scenarios. Tracheal rings 2-4 are used for formal tracheostomy.

questions.stem:
A 4-year-old child presents with high fever, drooling, stridor, and is sitting forward in the tripod position. A diagnosis of epiglottitis is suspected.

Why must throat examination be avoided before securing the airway?
options: The child may bite the examiner
Examination is painful and distressing
Stimulation of the inflamed epiglottis or gag reflex may precipitate complete laryngospasm and total airway obstruction
Throat examination may introduce further bacteria into the inflamed tissue
The child is too young to cooperate with examination
questions.explanation: In epiglottitis, the epiglottis is severely inflamed and oedematous. Any stimulation can precipitate complete laryngeal spasm and total airway obstruction. Management: keep the child calm in the position they choose, call senior anaesthetics and ENT urgently, secure the airway in a controlled theatre environment. Then give IV ceftriaxone.

questions.stem: The portal vein is formed posterior to the neck of the pancreas. Which two veins unite to form it?
options: Superior mesenteric vein and inferior mesenteric vein
Superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein
Splenic vein and left gastric vein
Inferior mesenteric vein and left renal vein
Superior mesenteric vein and right gastric vein
questions.explanation: The portal vein is formed posterior to the neck of the pancreas at approximately L2 by the union of the superior mesenteric vein and the splenic vein (which also receives the inferior mesenteric vein). Portal hypertension (e.g. from cirrhosis) causes portosystemic anastomoses — oesophageal varices, caput medusae, haemorrhoids.

questions.stem:
A 70-year-old man develops pericardial tamponade following a myocardial infarction complicated by ventricular free wall rupture.

What are the three components of Beck's triad, and what is the emergency treatment?
options: Tachycardia, raised JVP, fever — treated with antibiotics
Hypotension, raised JVP (distended neck veins), muffled heart sounds — treated with pericardiocentesis
Bradycardia, hypotension, confusion — treated with atropine
Raised JVP, peripheral oedema, ascites — treated with diuretics
Pleuritic chest pain, friction rub, ST changes — treated with NSAIDs
questions.explanation: Beck's triad of cardiac tamponade: (1) hypotension (compressed ventricles cannot fill); (2) raised JVP/distended neck veins (impaired venous return); (3) muffled/distant heart sounds. Additional signs: pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg fall in systolic BP on inspiration), electrical alternans on ECG. Emergency treatment: pericardiocentesis — even 20-30 mL of drainage can restore haemodynamics.

questions.stem: The hepatic arterial supply enters the porta hepatis. Which vessel provides the common hepatic artery, and what is the embryological basis for the area it supplies?
options: Superior mesenteric artery — supplies midgut derivatives
Inferior mesenteric artery — supplies hindgut derivatives
Coeliac trunk — supplies foregut derivatives including the liver, stomach, spleen, and pancreas
Abdominal aorta directly — supplies all abdominal organs
Internal iliac artery — supplies pelvic viscera
questions.explanation: The coeliac trunk (arising from the aorta at T12) gives three branches: left gastric, splenic, and common hepatic arteries. It supplies the foregut — from the lower oesophagus to the second part of the duodenum — including the liver, gallbladder, stomach, spleen, and pancreas. The SMA supplies the midgut; the IMA supplies the hindgut.

questions.stem:
A 65-year-old woman undergoes left hemicolectomy for sigmoid colon cancer. The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery.

What is the territory supplied by the IMA, and what collateral supply protects the bowel proximal to its origin?
options: Terminal ileum to splenic flexure — SMA collaterals via ileocolic artery
Splenic flexure to upper/mid rectum — anastomoses with SMA via the marginal artery of Drummond and arc of Riolan
Entire colon — no collateral supply exists
Rectum only — superior rectal artery with no collateral supply
Sigmoid colon only — direct branches from the aorta provide collateral supply
questions.explanation: The IMA supplies the large bowel from the splenic flexure to the upper rectum (left colic, sigmoid, superior rectal arteries). Collateral supply is provided by anastomoses with SMA branches via the marginal artery of Drummond and the arc of Riolan. The rectum also receives the middle and inferior rectal arteries from the internal iliac artery.

questions.stem: The common carotid artery bifurcates in the neck. At which vertebral level does this bifurcation typically occur, and what are the two important sensory structures found at the bifurcation?
options: C2 — carotid sinus and carotid body
C4 (upper border of thyroid cartilage) — carotid sinus (baroreceptor) and carotid body (chemoreceptor)
C6 (cricoid cartilage level) — superior cervical ganglion and carotid body
C3 — carotid sinus and thyroid gland
C7 — carotid sinus and aortic arch baroreceptors
questions.explanation: The common carotid artery bifurcates at C4 (the level of the upper border of the thyroid cartilage). At the bifurcation: the carotid sinus (baroreceptor in the wall of the internal carotid artery, afferents via CN IX) and the carotid body (chemoreceptor detecting O2, CO2, and pH changes, also CN IX afferents). Carotid sinus massage slows heart rate via the baroreflex.

questions.stem: The vertebral arteries unite to form the basilar artery. What does this vessel supply, and at which anatomical landmark do the vertebral arteries unite?
options: Anterior cerebral hemispheres — at the circle of Willis at the level of the optic chiasm
Brainstem, cerebellum, and posterior cerebral cortex — at the pontomedullary junction
Middle cerebral territory — at the level of the carotid siphon
Entire brain — at the foramen magnum
Occipital lobe only — at the tentorial notch
questions.explanation: The vertebral arteries enter the skull through the foramen magnum, then unite at the pontomedullary junction to form the basilar artery. It supplies the pons (via pontine perforating arteries), cerebellum (via AICA and SCA), and terminates by bifurcating into posterior cerebral arteries supplying the occipital lobes. Basilar artery occlusion can cause locked-in syndrome.

questions.stem:
A red artery and purple vein pass through the adductor canal. Below the inguinal ligament, the femoral artery gives off its largest branch in the thigh.

What is this largest branch, and what does it primarily supply?
options: Anterior tibial artery — anterior compartment of the leg
Profunda femoris (deep femoral artery) — all three compartments of the thigh via perforating branches, and the femoral head via circumflex femoral arteries
Popliteal artery — knee joint and leg
Obturator artery — medial thigh and hip joint
Lateral circumflex femoral artery — hip joint only
questions.explanation: The profunda femoris (deep femoral artery) is the largest branch of the femoral artery, arising posterolaterally about 4 cm below the inguinal ligament. It is the main blood supply to the thigh via perforating branches. It also gives medial and lateral circumflex femoral arteries — retinacular branches supply the femoral head and are at risk in femoral neck fractures causing avascular necrosis.

questions.stem:
A patient sustains a stab wound at the wrist, severing the median nerve.

(a) Name two specific muscles in the hand that would be paralysed.
(b) Describe the pattern of sensory loss.
(c) Name one clinical test to confirm the diagnosis.
questions.answer: (a) Any two of: abductor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, flexor pollicis brevis (superficial head), lumbricals 1 and 2. (b) Sensory loss over the palmar surface of the lateral 3.5 digits (thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger) and the dorsal surface of their distal phalanges. (c) Any one of: Tinel's test (tapping over flexor retinaculum reproduces paraesthesia); Phalen's test (sustained wrist flexion for 60s reproduces symptoms); testing abductor pollicis brevis against resistance.
questions.explanation: The median nerve enters the palm through the carpal tunnel. Its recurrent (thenar) branch supplies the thenar eminence muscles. Thenar wasting and the ape hand deformity (inability to oppose the thumb) are the hallmark clinical signs.

questions.stem:
(a) Name the two terminal divisions of the sciatic nerve.
(b) For each division, name the compartment of the leg it supplies and give two muscles it innervates.
(c) State at which level the sciatic nerve typically divides.
questions.answer: (a) Tibial nerve and common fibular (peroneal) nerve. (b) Tibial nerve: posterior compartment — gastrocnemius, soleus, tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, flexor hallucis longus. Common fibular nerve: anterior compartment (via deep fibular) — tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus; lateral compartment (via superficial fibular) — fibularis longus and brevis. (c) The sciatic nerve typically divides at the upper angle of the popliteal fossa (approximately the lower third of the thigh), though the level is variable.
questions.explanation: The tibial nerve continues through the popliteal fossa and posterior leg, then passes through the tarsal tunnel (Tom Dick And Very Nervous Harry) to supply the plantar muscles. The common fibular nerve winds around the fibular neck (vulnerable to injury).

questions.stem:
(a) Name the three structures within the carotid sheath.
(b) State the difference in course between the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves.
(c) What would be the consequence of bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve damage?
questions.answer: (a) Common carotid artery (medial), internal jugular vein (lateral), vagus nerve CN X (posterior, between artery and vein). (b) Left RLN: loops under the arch of the aorta at the ligamentum arteriosum, then ascends in the tracheo-oesophageal groove. Right RLN: loops under the right subclavian artery, then ascends in the tracheo-oesophageal groove. The left has a longer intrathoracic course and is vulnerable to mediastinal pathology (lung cancer, aortic aneurysm). (c) Bilateral RLN damage paralyses all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except cricothyroid, causing both vocal folds to fall to the midline — stridor — life-threatening airway obstruction requiring emergency tracheostomy.
questions.explanation: The RLN supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT cricothyroid (supplied by the external laryngeal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve). The posterior cricoarytenoid is the only abductor of the vocal folds — its bilateral paralysis causes the respiratory emergency.

questions.stem:
A patient presents with bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, and loss of bladder and bowel control following a large central lumbar disc herniation.

(a) Name the syndrome.
(b) Below which vertebral level does the spinal cord end, and what structure fills the canal below this level?
(c) State the urgency of management and the investigation of choice.
questions.answer: (a) Cauda equina syndrome. (b) The spinal cord ends at L1-L2 as the conus medullaris. Below this level the spinal canal contains the cauda equina — a collection of lumbar and sacral nerve roots descending within the dural sac. (c) This is a neurosurgical emergency — urgent MRI of the lumbar spine must be obtained immediately. Surgical decompression should be performed as soon as possible, ideally within 24-48 hours of symptom onset, to maximise recovery of bladder and bowel function.
questions.explanation: The cauda equina (horse's tail) contains the L2-S5 nerve roots. The cardinal features are bladder dysfunction (urinary retention or overflow incontinence), bowel dysfunction, saddle anaesthesia (S3-S5 dermatomes), and bilateral leg weakness. Lumbar puncture is safely performed at L3/4 or L4/5 because the cord has ended.

questions.stem:
(a) Name the three branches of the coeliac trunk.
(b) Trace the complete arterial pathway from the aortic arch to the radial artery at the wrist on the right side.
(c) At which specific point does the axillary artery become the brachial artery?
questions.answer: (a) Left gastric artery, splenic artery, common hepatic artery. (b) Aortic arch → brachiocephalic trunk → right subclavian artery → (at lateral border of 1st rib) axillary artery → (at lower border of teres major) brachial artery → (in cubital fossa) bifurcates into radial artery (lateral forearm → anatomical snuffbox → deep palmar arch) and ulnar artery (medial forearm → Guyon's canal → superficial palmar arch). (c) The axillary artery becomes the brachial artery at the lower border of teres major.
questions.explanation: On the left: the subclavian artery arises directly from the aortic arch. On the right: it arises from the brachiocephalic trunk. The axillary artery is divided into three parts by pectoralis minor. The radial artery passes through the anatomical snuffbox forming the deep palmar arch (dominant). The ulnar artery passes through Guyon's canal forming the superficial palmar arch (dominant).

questions.stem:
A 22-year-old falls on an outstretched hand. He has tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox and pain on axial loading of the thumb.

Which carpal bone is most likely fractured, and what serious complication may result from a fracture through its proximal waist?
options: Lunate — Kienbock disease (avascular necrosis of the lunate)
Scaphoid — avascular necrosis of the proximal pole due to its retrograde blood supply
Trapezium — non-union at the base of the thumb
Capitate — compression of the median nerve
Triquetrum — ulnar nerve injury
questions.explanation: The scaphoid is the most commonly fractured carpal bone, typically from a fall on an outstretched hand with forced dorsiflexion. Its blood supply enters distally from the radial artery — fractures of the proximal waist or proximal pole interrupt this retrograde supply, risking avascular necrosis of the proximal fragment. Plain X-ray may be normal initially — MRI or repeat X-ray at 10-14 days is required if scaphoid fracture is suspected.
